title: After the AP Computer Science Data Science Capstone
text: This resource is intended for high school computer science teachers seeking to extend student learning beyond AP Computer Science courses. It provides a teacher-facing guide for the After the AP Capstone.The materials for the capstone are designed for students who have completed AP Computer Science Principles or AP Computer Science A, or who have comparable experience with text-based programming.

title: Introduction
text:
Purpose
This resource is a teacher guide for a data investigation project designed to extend student learning beyond AP Computer Science courses. The guide supports high school computer science teachers in planning and facilitating a data science project that builds on students’ prior programming experience and does not assume prior coursework in statistics or data science.
It explains the structure of the project, the dataset used, and the types of analyses students are expected to pursue. The project is intended for students who have completed AP Computer Science Principles or AP Computer Science A, or who have comparable experience with text-based programming.

title: Introduction
text:
Data Science Tools Used



This project uses Python with the pandas library for data manipulation (McKinney 2010) and the matplotlib library for data visualization (Hunter 2007).

title: Introduction
text:
Dataset
The project uses data from the General Social Survey (GSS). The GSS is primarily funded by the National Science Foundation (NSF) and conducted by the National Opinion Research Center (NORC), a non-partisan research organization. The GSS collects data on a variety of topics in contemporary American society to monitor and explain trends in opinions, attitudes, and behaviors.
The GSS contains a standard core of demographic, behavioral, and attitudinal questions, plus topics of special interest. Among the topics covered are education, civil liberties, national spending priorities, psychological well-being, and social mobility. It allows researchers to examine the structure and functioning of society in general, as well as the role played by relevant subgroups.




Hunter, John D. 2007. “Matplotlib: A 2D Graphics Environment.” Computing in Science & Engineering 9 (3): 90–95. https://matplotlib.org/.


McKinney, Wes. 2010. “Data Structures for Statistical Computing in Python.” Proceedings of the 9th Python in Science Conference, 56–61. https://doi.org/10.25080/Majora-92bf1922-00a.

title: Dataset
text:
General Social Survey
The project uses data from the General Social Survey (GSS). The GSS is primarily funded by the National Science Foundation (NSF) and conducted by the National Opinion Research Center (NORC), a non-partisan research organization. The GSS collects data on a variety of topics in contemporary American society to monitor and explain trends in opinions, attitudes, and behaviors.
The GSS contains a standard core of demographic, behavioral, and attitudinal questions, plus topics of special interest. Among the topics covered are education, civil liberties, national spending priorities, psychological well-being, and social mobility. It allows researchers to examine the structure and functioning of society in general, as well as the role played by relevant subgroups.

title: Dataset
text:
About the General Social Survey
The GSS is a nationally representative series of cross-sectional interviews conducted in the United States since 1972. By adapting questions from earlier surveys, the GSS enables comparisons across decades. Originally proposed by James A. Davis, the survey has been administered by NORC at the University of Chicago and funded by the NSF since its inception. The GSS is currently designed by a set of Principal Investigators, with guidance from a Board of researchers.
In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, the 2021 GSS implemented methodological changes, including a shift to address-based sampling with a push-to-web, self-administered questionnaire. The 2022 GSS was designed as a bridge between the traditional face-to-face data collection used from 1972–2018 and the web-based approach introduced in 2021. It retained many questionnaire changes from the web mode while returning to a mixed-mode design that included face-to-face, web, and telephone data collection. The 2022 GSS was structured to facilitate comparison with the 2018 GSS and represents the first stage of a broader transition to a mixed-mode survey.

title: Dataset
text:
The Project Dataset
The dataset used for this project is a subset of the full GSS dataset, downloaded on December 31, 2025. The original dataset contained 46 variables and 75,699 observations.

Data Preparation & Cleaning
The dataset used in this project is based on the original GSS data but includes pre-processing steps to support classroom use and analysis.
The following changes were made prior to student use:

A subset of variables and observations relevant to the project was selected.
Non-substantive response codes (e.g., .i:  Inapplicable, .n:  No answer) were kept in their original form; some were removed or recoded during cleaning.
The variable age_group was created.
Variables originally labeled cohort and year were renamed to birth_year and survey_year.
The variable wwwhr was recoded from categorical response values to numeric.
The dataset was filtered to include respondents age 51 and under.

These steps were taken to reduce complexity while preserving the structure and integrity of the original survey data.
Teachers and students should be aware that the dataset is not a raw extract from the GSS, but a prepared version intended for instructional use.


Tidy and Tame Data Considerations
The project dataset has been prepared to follow principles of tidy data (Wickham 2014) and tame data (Kim, Ismay, and Chunn 2018) to support exploratory analysis.
In this dataset:

Each row represents a single institution.
Each column represents a single variable.
Each cell contains a single, well-defined value.

Variables were also selected and organized to make the dataset tame, meaning, manageable, interpretable, and appropriate for classroom analysis. This includes limiting the number of variables, using consistent naming conventions, and providing variables that are meaningful for comparative analysis across institutions.
These design choices reduce unnecessary data wrangling and allow students to focus on asking questions, exploring patterns, and interpreting results, while still working with authentic, real-world data.




Kim, Albert Y., Chester Ismay, and Jennifer Chunn. 2018. “The Fivethirtyeight r Package: ’Tame Data’ Principles for Introductory Statistics and Data Science Courses.” Technology Innovations in Statistics Education 11 (1). https://escholarship.org/uc/item/0rx1231m.


Wickham, Hadley. 2014. “Tidy Data.” Journal of Statistical Software 59. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v059.i10.

title: Variables
text:
Missing & Non-Substantive Codes
This section describes codes used to represent missing, inapplicable, or non-substantive responses in the GSS. Missing and non-substantive codes appear in survey data when a question does not apply to a respondent, was skipped, or could not be answered. These values are not errors.
Some missing values occur because a question was never asked (for example, it was not included in a particular survey year), while others reflect meaningful responses such as “don’t know” or “not applicable.”
When analyzing the data, these codes should be handled intentionally. In most cases, they should be excluded from summaries and visualizations, rather than treated as valid responses.








Code
Meaning
Notes & When It Appears




NaN
Missing value (numeric)
Pandas missing value; appears in numeric columns


.i:  Inapplicable
Inapplicable
Question does not apply due to skip patterns or survey design


.n:  No answer
No answer
Question was asked, but no valid response recorded


.d:  Do not Know/Cannot Choose
Don’t know / cannot choose
Respondent could not or would not provide a substantive answer


.s:  Skipped on Web
Skipped on web
Respondent skipped the question in web-based surveys


.y:  Not available in this year
Not asked in this year
Variable not included in that survey year









Working with Missing Values



Missing and non-substantive codes are not errors. They indicate when a question did not apply, was skipped, or could not be answered. These values should usually be excluded from summaries and visualizations.

title: Helpful Notebook Tips
text:
Introduction to Jupyter Notebook
A Jupyter Notebook is a web-based interactive computing environment that supports programming in Python, R, and other languages (Stokes and Harding 2025). In this project, you will use Jupyter Notebooks to write code, explore data, and document your analysis.

title: Helpful Notebook Tips
text:
Overview of a Jupyter Notebook
Jupyter notebooks allow users to create and share documents that combine code, equations, visualizations, and formatted text (Stokes and Harding 2025). They are commonly used for data science because users can write and run code step by step, document their thinking, and view results directly alongside the code that produced them.






Avoid Multiple Tabs



Avoid opening multiple notebooks in separate tabs, as this can lead to memory overload, forcing you to restart your session.

title: Helpful Notebook Tips
text:
Code & Text Cells
A Jupyter Notebook has three types of cells. For this project, you will use only two:

Code cells for writing and running Python code
Markdown or text cells for entering written responses.


Running Cells in a Jupyter Notebook
In a Jupyter Notebook, you run cells one at a time to see code output or display formatted text. To run a cell, click inside the cell and then click the Run button in the toolbar at the top of the notebook.



Run button in the Jupyter toolbar


You can also use keyboard shortcuts:

PCs and Chromebooks: Hold Ctrl and press Enter
Macs: Hold Command (⌘) and press Enter



Adding Cells
Use the + button in the top toolbar to add a new cell.



+ button in the Jupyter toolbar


Then set the cell type as needed:



Code or Markdown button in the Jupyter toolbar



Choose Code for code
Choose Markdown for text



Running All Cells from Top Down
The Save button saves your code and text, but it does not save data stored in memory. Whenever you reopen a notebook, you should re-run all code cells from the top.
To do this, go to Kernel → Restart & Run All.






Restart & Run All



This option restarts the notebook and runs every cell in order from top to bottom. If a code cell encounters an error, execution will stop and later cells will not run.





Restart & Run All button in the Jupyter toolbar






Stokes, David J., and Mahmoud Harding. 2025. “A Project-Based Approach to Introductory R and Python for Data Science.” https://djstokes02.github.io/IntroRPython4DS/.

title: Useful Python Commands
text:
Variables and Assignment
Python variables are created by assignment. A valid Python variable name must start with a letter or an underscore, use only letters, numbers, and underscores, and can’t be a reserved Python keyword.
Syntax
variable_name = value
Example

x = 10
x

10




Data Structures

Lists ([ ])
Lists store ordered collections of values. Items are accessed by index, starting at 0.
Syntax
my_list = [item1, item2, item3]
my_list[index]
Example

letters = ["A", "B", "C", "D", "E"]

print(letters[0])
print(letters[1])
print(letters[2])
print(letters[3])
print(letters[4])

A
B
C
D
E




List Slices (:)
Slices select a range of values from a list. The ending index is not included.
Syntax
my_list[start:end]
my_list[:end]
my_list[start:]
Example

print(letters[0:3])
print(letters[:3])

['A', 'B', 'C']
['A', 'B', 'C']


Example

print(letters[-3:])

['C', 'D', 'E']








Slice reminder




[:3] and [:3] both mean items at index positions 0–2
[-3:] means the last three items





Dictionaries ({ })
Dictionaries store key–value pairs. Values are accessed using keys.
Syntax
my_dict = {key1: value1, key2: value2}
my_dict[key1]
my_dict[key2]
Example (string keys)

print(exchange_rates)
print(exchange_rates["EUR"])
print(exchange_rates["JPY"])
print(exchange_rates["GBP"])

{'EUR': 0.92, 'JPY': 148.5, 'GBP': 0.79}
0.92
148.5
0.79


Example (numeric keys)

print(phase_changes)
print(phase_changes[-1])
print(phase_changes[0])
print(phase_changes[100])

{-1: 'Below freezing', 0: 'Freezing point of water', 100: 'Boiling point of water'}
Below freezing
Freezing point of water
Boiling point of water








Lists vs Dictionaries




Use a list when order matters (index-based).
Use a dictionary when you need to look up values by a meaningful label (key-based).






Functions

print()
The print() function displays output.
Syntax
print(value)
Example

print("Hello!")
print(x)

Hello!
10




len()
The len() function returns the number of items in a collection.
Syntax
len(collection)
Example

print(letters)
print(len(letters))

['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']
5




set()
A set stores only unique values.
Syntax
set(collection)
Example

grade = ["9th grade",
    "10th grade",
    "11th grade",
    "12th grade",
    "9th grade",
    "10th grade",
    "11th grade",
    "12th grade",
    "11th grade",
    "10th grade"]
 
set(grade)

{'10th grade', '11th grade', '12th grade', '9th grade'}








When to use set()



Use set() to identify distinct values in a list.





Control Structures

for
A for loop repeats an action for each item in a collection.
Syntax
for item in collection:
    print(item)
Example

for item in letters[-3:]:
    print(item)

C
D
E




with open()
This pattern reads a file and automatically closes it when finished.
Syntax
my_list = []

with open("path/to/file.txt", "r") as file:
    for line in file:
        clean = line.strip()
        my_list.append(clean)
Example

education = []

with open("data/education.txt", "r") as file:
    for line in file:
        newline = line.strip()
        education.append(newline)

education[:10]

['11th grade',
 '4 years of college',

 '3 years of college',
 '5 years of college',
 '11th grade',
 '12th grade',
 '12th grade',
 '6 years of college',
 '6 years of college']





Methods
Methods are functions that belong to an object and are called using dot notation.

.strip()
Removes extra whitespace (such as spaces or "\n") from the beginning and end of a string.
Syntax
string.strip()
Example

text = "  hello\n"
clean = text.strip()

print(clean)

hello








Why .strip() matters



Text files often contain invisible characters such as "\n" at the end of each line. .strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace from strings.




.append()
The .append() method adds one item to the end of a list.
Syntax
my_list.append(value)
Example

print(letters)

letters.append("F")
print(letters)

['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']
['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F']

title: Useful Python Commands
text:
Notebook 3: Visualizing Distributions

Matplotlib
matplotlib.pyplot is a Python library used for creating visualizations such as bar charts, histograms, and boxplots.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt loads the plotting library and assigns it the alias plt.
Syntax
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
Example

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


as plt creates a shortcut (alias).
plt is the standard alias for matplotlib.



.plot.bar()
Bar charts show the distribution of a categorical variable and are created using .plot.bar().
Syntax
dataframe["column_name"].value_counts().plot.bar()
Example

gss["age_group"].value_counts().plot.bar()
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Age Groups")
plt.show();














Using .value_counts()



.value_counts() counts each category, which provides the values used to create a bar chart.


Example

pd.crosstab(deg_hap_df["degree"], deg_hap_df["happy"]).plot.bar()

plt.xlabel("Degree")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Happiness Levels by Education Level")
plt.legend(title="Happiness Level")
plt.show();









Labels and Titles
Use these functions to label and display the visualization.



Function
Purpose




plt.xlabel()
Labels the \(x-\)axis


plt.ylabel()
Labels the \(y-\)axis


plt.title()
Adds a descriptive title


plt.legend()
Adds a descriptive legend title


plt.show()
Displays the completed plot






.hist()
Histograms show the distribution of a numeric variable and are created using .hist().
Syntax
dataframe["numeric_column"].hist()
Example

gss["age"].hist()
plt.xlabel("Age")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Age")
plt.show();








Example

gss["age"].hist(edgecolor="white", grid=False)
plt.xlabel("Age")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Age")
plt.show();














Improving readability



Adding edgecolor="white" outlines the bins, and grid=False removes the background grid.




.boxplot()
Boxplots show the distribution of a numeric variable across categories.
Syntax
dataframe.boxplot(column="numeric_column", by="category_column")
Example

gss.boxplot(column="tvhours", by="age_group", vert=False, grid=False)
plt.title("Distribution of TV Viewing by Age Group")
plt.suptitle("")
plt.xlabel("Hours Spent Watching Television")
plt.ylabel("")
plt.show();














Controlling orientation



vert=False displays the boxplot horizontally.



Title
Use this function to label the visualization.



Function
Purpose




plt.suptitle()
Controls the overall figure title

title: Useful Python Commands
text:
Notebook 4: Visualizing Trends

.plot()
Line charts show how a numerical variable changes over an ordered variable (such as time) and are created using .plot().
Syntax
grouped = dataframe.groupby("time_column")
grouped["numeric_column"].aggregation_function().plot()
Example

grps_survey_year = gss.groupby("survey_year")

grps_survey_year["wwwhr"].mean().round(2).dropna().plot()
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Time (Hours)")
plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
plt.title("Mean Internet Usage per Week by Survey Year")
plt.show();














Why grouping first matters



Line charts require summarized data. .groupby() should be used before plotting to calculate summary values over time.



Gridlines



Function
Purpose




plt.grid()
Adds or removes gridlines









Improving readability



plt.grid(axis= y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6) adds light horizontal gridlines.


Example

wwwhr_years = range(2000, 2024, 2)

grps_survey_year["wwwhr"].mean().round(2).dropna().plot(marker="o")
plt.xticks(wwwhr_years)
plt.xlabel("")
plt.ylabel("Time (Hours)")
plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
plt.title("Mean Internet Usage per Week by Survey Year")
plt.show();














Markers



marker="o" adds a circle at each data point on the line.




\(x-\)ticks







Function
Purpose




plt.xticks()
Controls the tick labels and positions on the \(x-\)axis









Using range()



range(2000, 2024, 2) generates values from 2000 up to (but not including) 2024, stepping by 2.





.unstack()
After grouping by two variables (time and group), .unstack() moves the group level into columns so each group becomes its own line.
Syntax
grouped = dataframe.groupby(["time_column", "group_column"])
grouped["numeric_column"].mean().dropna().round(2).unstack()
Example

grps_age_groups = gss.groupby(["survey_year", "age_group"])
grps_age_groups["yearsjob"].mean().dropna().round(2).unstack()

title: Resources
text:
Video Resources
The General Social Survey (GSS) YouTube channel has an educational video series designed to support instructors, students, and researchers using GSS data.
The following videos provide an overview of the General Social Survey (GSS), from survey design and sampling to data access and instructional use.

title: Resources
text:
Video
Description




Introduction to the GSS
Brief overview of the GSS, including its history, key stakeholders, and selected academic contributions. Positions the GSS as a widely used data resource for undergraduate instruction and research.


GSS Project Process & Life Cycle
Explains the full lifecycle of the GSS survey, including proposal review, testing, programming, pilot studies, data collection, cleaning, dissemination, and user support.


The GSS Structure & Basics
Describes the structure of the GSS, including core content, experiments, cross-sectional and panel studies, ISSP modules, and the balloting process. Covers basic skills for navigating, downloading, and interpreting GSS data.


Sampling in the GSS
Explains how representative samples are drawn for the GSS and introduces key sampling concepts used in the survey.


Total Survey Error in the GSS
Discusses sources of error in survey data, including nonresponse error, coverage error, sampling error, and measurement error.


GSS Wording Experiments
Provides examples of how question wording affects responses, highlighting GSS contributions to understanding measurement effects (e.g., national spending priority items).


The GSS Data Explorer
Tutorial on using the GSS Data Explorer, including online analytics, visualizations, and features introduced in Data Explorer 2.0.


GSS for Educators
Targeted to instructors; shares ideas for incorporating GSS data into lesson plans and classroom activities.


GSS for STEM
Focuses on working with data beyond survey responses, including paradata and linked datasets such as the death index.


Taking a Deep Dive into GSS Content
Overview of major variable groups and content areas, highlighting resources and recent trends relevant to researchers and educators.

title: Partners
text:
CourseKata
The After the AP Data Science Capstone uses Jupyter notebooks hosted on CKHub, developed by CourseKata. If this challenge sparked your interest, contact us CourseKata.org for more classroom-ready resources like these, including materials you can use throughout the year to supplement your regular curriculum.
We’re a nonprofit team of educators and learning scientists who build both full-year and supplementary statistics and data science curricula using the same kinds of Jupyter notebooks, real datasets, and modeling tools used in the challenge.
Our technology and curriculum are designed to bring authentic data science practices into classrooms in ways that are teaching-friendly, meaningful, and equitable. Our research on lowering barriers to participation for students historically marginalized in STEM directly informs how we design our learning materials so more students can engage in mathematics that matters in the modern world.

University of Minnesota Department of Educational Psychology



Lab Advancing Statistics Education Research (LASER)


We are working with CourseKata to evaluate the After the AP Data Science Capstone. From providing feedback on materials to surveying participants and instructors, our team will provide insights about this project to help CourseKata improve and refine this for future participants.
Our team consists of professors and graduate students within the Educational Psychology department at the University of Minnesota. Our research focuses on the teaching and learning of statistics and data science. You can find many of our current and former projects at our research lab website: Lab Advancing Statistics Education Research (LASER).
